event: 59 years old
female
found to have a segment-V space-occupying lesion in the liver
suspected of being liver cancer
transarterial embolization was performed
began taking sorafenib
stopped taking sorafenib due to dizziness, skin ulcers, and other symptoms
computed tomography (CT) scan found that the lesions in liver segment V had enlarged
radiation therapy was performed
PTV5400cGy/18f, and a dose rate of 0.6-1 Gy/min for a total of seven radiotherapy treatments
Piggyback LT was performed
had a history of hepatitis B
received entecavir
hepatitis B virus (HBV) serology test: HBsAg was 113.23I U/mL (ref: 0--0.05)
anti-HBs was 0 mIU/mL (ref: 0--10)
HBeAg was 0.08 PEI µ/mL (ref: 0--0.5)
anti-HBe was >4.4 PEI µ/mL (ref: 0--0.2)
anti-HBc was 8.26 PEI µ/mL (ref: 0--0.9)
serological tests for human immunodeficiency virus, hepatitis A, and hepatitis C were all negative
transplantation process was smooth
developed acute renal failure and a hematoma around the liver
received intravenous administration of hepatitis B immunoglobulin
on postoperative day (POD)-1, the patient received immunosuppressive drugs
maintain the tacrolimus blood level at ~10 ng/mL
active bleeding in the abdominal cavity ceased and renal function gradually recovered
pathological analyses confirmed the diagnosis of HCC
massive tumor necrosis was observed
liver function began to improve
aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) levels all returned to normal
developed regular and repeated fevers of 37--39°C
procalcitonin (PCT) levels began to rise to 10.3 ng/mL (ref: 0--0.05)
serum PCT levels dropped to 3 ng/mL
blood cultures were negative
cytomegalovirus, Epstein--Barr virus (EBV) were negative
fever did not subside, and obscure red spots were also observed on the patient’s chest
no symptoms such as itching, and the Nikolsky sign was negative
changed the tacrolimus administration to sirolimus and added mycophenolate mofetil
a sputum culture suggested the presence of Acinetobacter baumannii and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections
patient’s rash advanced into erythematous macules and papules
spread to the limbs, palms, neck, and face
oral examination revealed white ulcers on both sides of the buccal mucosa and lips
severe bone marrow suppression was observed
white blood cell (WBC) count of 0.86 × 109/L (ref: 3.5--9.5)
platelet (PLT) count of 35 × 109/L (ref: 125--350)
hemoglobin (HGB) level of 70 g/L (ref: 130--175)
transferred to the intensive care unit
a dermatologist hypothesized that the rash may be a drug-related adverse reaction
gamma globulin administration (2500 mg, 3 days)
performed a skin biopsy on the patient’s left chest and performed fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) of the peripheral blood
abdominal incision split and was sutured again
pathology results confirmed the diagnosis of HCC
necrosis accounted for ~90%, while the surviving tumor accounted for ~10%
an MVI grade of M0, and no tumor involvement at the surgical margin
patient's laboratory indicators began to change after LT
AST, ALT, GGT, and ALP levels all returned to normal
PCT levels began to rise to 10.3 ng/mL
serum PCT levels dropped to 3 ng/mL
patient's rash advanced into erythematous macules and papules
spread to the limbs, palms, neck, and face
oral examination revealed white ulcers on both sides of the buccal mucosa and lips
severe bone marrow suppression was observed
white blood cell (WBC) count of 0.86 × 109/L
platelet (PLT) count of 35 × 109/L
hemoglobin (HGB) level of 70 g/L
performed a bone marrow aspiration to determine the cause of the bone marrow suppression
bone marrow pathology report revealed no special lesions in the morphology of granulocytes, red blood cells, or megakaryocytes
bone marrow cell morphology examination revealed that the bone marrow cells were proliferating and active
megakaryocyte production was reduced, and platelet levels decreased
performed FISH analysis of the peripheral blood followed by flow cytometry detected 3% donor lymphocytes
skin biopsy specimens exhibited epidermal dyskeratosis, basic vacuolization, and lymphocytic infiltrates
diagnosed with grade-1 acute lt-GVHD
differential diagnoses include bacterial, fungal, and viral infections, drug reactions, toxic epidermal necrolysis, and hemophagocytic syndrome
blood culture was negative at the onset of the rash
recipient had no history of allergies to drugs
serological tests of the virus were negative
no macrophages phagocytosing neutrophils or platelets were observed in the bone marrow biopsy
the collective findings, together with the clinical course, were consistent with the diagnosis of lt-GVHD
analysis of serum T-lymphocyte subsets revealed that the ratio of CD4:CD8 was reversed
concentration of serum immunoglobin M was reduced to 0.3 g/L
despite continuous platelet transfusion and the use of thrombopoietin (TPO), the patient’s PLT count dropped to 3.2 × 109/L
